title: 2026 02 18 FEC
displayTitle: 2026-02-18 - Finance and Expenditure Committee - Briefing on the response to the inquiry into banking competition
topic: Briefing on the response to the inquiry into banking competition
description: Wednesday 18 February 2026| Finance and Expenditure Committee Briefing on the response to the inquiry into banking competition Dosh Shane Marsh, Chief Executive Kiwibank Susan Peterson, Chair Steve Jurkovich, Chief Executive Non-bank Deposit Takers Association Daniel McGrath, Xceda, CEO John Healye, Wairarapa Building Society
descriptionSummary: Briefing on the response to the inquiry into banking competition. Dosh. Shane Marsh, Chief Executive

chunks.text: We've got, uh, Shane Marsh here, chief Executive of dos, uh, online. Welcome, Shane. You've got 15 minutes. If you can give us some introductory remarks. Thank you for your written submission. And then we'll go to questions. So, good morning. Good morning. Thank you, uh, for having me along this morning. Uh, so, uh, we've got really two, uh, start my video, two key elements of the update from DOS in terms of how we are working to, uh, improve competition in the the banking sector.

Uh, the first is that, um, DOS believes that to add value to Kiwis, uh, in banking services, the best approach is to build an integrated ecosystem. Over the last year, we've added, uh, Westpac Visa, and most recently, one New Zealand, uh, to a broader ecosystem of, uh, organizations that are working together to provide innovative financial services to Kiwis.

The most recent one, uh, one New Zealand launched a couple weeks ago. Uh, it really is an, an innovation, uh, in New Zealand. Uh, together we are offering, um, new value to customers, um, not only in terms of the financial services they receive, such as, uh, uh, great interest rates, innovative, uh, uh, value in terms of their home loans, uh, but also their card spend, but also enabling customers to get a faster access to the leading technology in terms of mobile phone devices, uh, very new in the market.

Um, there's a number of instances offshore where telecommunications works with fintechs and banking providers. Um, and there's been a lot of success from that as well. So what we're doing in New Zealand is really looking at how we can bring the best, uh, solutions that have worked offshore to New Zealand to add better value to Kiwis in New Zealand.

Um, the second topic is really around an update in terms of the application we made to the Reserve Bank, uh, for an application for a a banking license. Um, if we kind of step back for a bit, um, was, uh, originated out of, uh, Singapore, where myself, my co-founder, we were working in a market that had a lot of competition and banking, new services, innovation.

And we've seen that around the world, uh, from the UK with the likes of Monzo and in Australia, with the likes of UP bank, really bringing new digital services, innovation and competition and value to those, uh, countries and markets. Our approach has always been to look at what's working offshore and how do we bring that to New Zealand to offer better value in a market that, to be honest, has, has fallen behind, um, what we're seeing, um, been offered around the world.

The model that has been most successful in other countries has been, uh, in a transactional bank model. So a transactional bank model is where a new entrant offers, uh, accounts payments, uh, cards and savings to customers generally through, uh, a mobile app, um, and provides better value, um, better customer experience.

Um, and through that, the likes of Monzo have been able to scale rapidly, regularly, win, um, bank of the Year awards, um, and then look and at time as they scale and mature to add additional products. One thing that they're not offering is lending. With lending comes lending, uh, greater regulatory, uh, burden, uh, greater risk and greater capital requirements.

So it really is a, is a easy path, um, to, to scale a bank. And it's that model that we, uh, propose to the Reserve Bank that dos would adopt, um, in New Zealand, uh, to get a, a banking license, and then we would look to develop the business from there. Um, we relied on the definition of a bank, uh, in the, um, banking credential Division Act, uh, specifically, um, clause, uh, 73 1, which says that a bank consists of the borrowing and lending of money or the provision of other financial services or both.

And it was our interpretation that other financial services would include what we deemed to be kind of basic banking services, the type that your, uh, 18-year-old might take when they've getting their first job, which is, I need an account, I need to have my wages paid in, I want, might wanna save some money. Um, and I wanna make some, some payments perhaps on a, on a card.

Um, the Reserve Bank has declined that model. Um, the Reserve Bank deemed that in New Zealand, uh, their interpretation of the legislation is that, uh, lending is a mandatory requirement for banking in New Zealand. Um, that's a different position than what we see overseas.

Um, and we see that that is, is a relatively narrow and restrictive interpretation, um, of the legislation. Um, and it means that, uh, the transactional model for, for new entrant in New Zealand is not a path, um, to becoming a bank here in this, um, this country.

We respect the Reserve Bank in terms of their interpretation and expertise. We've continued to have dialogue with them, um, and we continue to consider whether, uh, what our best future model is. Um, we're open to resubmitting, um, and if we do so, that would be, um, by meeting the lending requirement. Um, but from our perspective, um, we have a flourishing business.

We're adding real value to Kiwis. Uh, we're growing and scaling with our partners, and so the best way forward is really depending on what's the most viable, uh, in this market, um, albeit the pathways that have been successful overseas are not available here. Okay. Thank you. Thank you, Mr. Marsh. We're gonna go to our deputy chair, Ryan Hamilton.

Uh, thanks Shane. Good to see you. And, um, good to see the progress you're making in, in this, um, innovative space. Yeah, disappointed, obviously to hear about your news on the Reserve Bank. I understand we had them in last week. They're still working on that bank definition stuff, so hopefully there'll be the opportunity for you to reapply. Um, you mentioned your submission, otherwise you might have to narrow your, your business model.

What would that, what would that look like? Is that quite prohibitive for you? Well, I think the main reason what we applied to be a bank is that, uh, with, uh, the bank, you, because you're regulated by the Reserve Bank and you've got the bank title of bank in your name, it brings a degree of trust and credibility that, uh, consumers in New Zealand can rely on so that they can have confidence that they can place their money and take services.

So we've been able to build that up, um, without that, but without doubt having that as, um, something we can stand behind so that we can compete in the banking market, we can talk about offering banking services, um, would be, uh, an advantage for us. So that's really the restriction is, um, there's obviously some restriction around some of the products we can offer in terms of lending and how we offer them, but, uh, the question is really, can you get to the point of competing with the likes of the top five banks, um, without that, um,

that position in the market. Thank you. And also, obviously, your relationship with One New Zealand, I assume has been, uh, helped the work of this committee, the work of the government on differentiating prepaid interchange pricings for fintechs. Can you maybe talk a bit about that and how that's looking and shaping up for you? Yeah, absolutely. So when, uh, the, the government was looking at, uh, interchange pricing, there was, uh, we had submitted that, uh, for the prepaid option that we offer.

Uh, the interchange we use, uh, we generate is used to add value to customers and to further grow our business. And so that pricing was left, um, as it was. What we've been able to do is to use that to offer a really strong, um, opposition and proposition the market together with one that effectively enables, uh, anyone who spends anyone who has a card to save up over time to, to generate their next, uh, phone upgrade.

And as we know in this market, with the cost of living crisis, it's really tough for people to be able to, uh, afford effectively a luxury item, but as we know, so critical to being, uh, in the leading position in terms of tech and, and access to the, the latest, um, devices. So look, it's a real big positive. Um, we're super excited to be working one nz.

They're a really customer focused organization, and we think this has, uh, got great, um, growth opportunities. Fantastic. Okay. Thank you. Okay, thank you. We're gonna go to, um, uh, Dr. Deborah Russell. Yeah. Um, I'm just, uh, kind of a little bit puzzled. I, I've missed something somewhere, but I'm looking at your app and your website, and it does say you offer home loans.

Yeah, sorry. So we, um, are effectively offering a broking service, um, exclusively with, uh, Westpac, uh, who's our, our partner in that. So, um, so effectively the offer is that if you, uh, take a, uh, home loan through dos, so we organize your home loan with Westpac, you'll get kind of the, the great rates and any cash back that may be available offered by Westpac.

But in addition to that, you get a free, um, iPhone 17 effectively. So it's a, um, it's a minute innovative, but, um, amazing offer in the market that we just launched two weeks ago. Right. Okay. So, but, and so, but that's not gonna be enough to meet that borrowing and lending of money criterion In itself.

'cause Westpac is doing the actual lending. You're just brokering. Yeah. So it could always be an option for us to bring that in house, um, and to, to lend. That's where a lot of the complexity, uh, comes about. It's access to low cost funding. It's the capital requirements that re relate to that and the risk, um, policies and management that comes on top of that.

So that's, that's all possible. Um, but it's quite a lot of complexity and burden to take on that is we believe, not necessary to be offering banking services. Um, and the way in which, uh, we've, um, approached it from a more of a transactional perspective. Okay. Thank you. Okay. Any further questions? Yeah.

Yes. Uh, Dr. David Wilson. Uh, Thanks Shane. Really good, uh, presentation. Thank you very much for that. Just, uh, wondering, do you offer your clients, um, transactional services internationally? So can you transfer money for them to other places? And if so, how? Yeah, so you can use your, um, card to make purchases if you're overseas, um, and you make purchases online, but we don't have international payments.

Yeah. Uh, offering with dos, um, I was once the head of international payments for the major bank, so it's a business I know very well. Um, however, it does come with a degree of, um, risk. Um, and that's, so that's something that's on our pipeline. But's, not something that we've rolled out at this stage. Thank you. One Very quick. Yes. Yep. No, we've got time. Just One very quick, because I'm looking at your, thanks, Shane.

We appreciate you being here. Um, you just put in, in your responses in your presentation that, um, you didn't, that the Reserve Bank said you didn't meet the capital requirements 'cause of the $5 million. Was that directly linked to the fact that they wanted you to be doing lending so your capital requirements would be a whole lot higher? Or is that a, is that just another reason they declined your application?

Yeah, I have really good questions because the, the primary reason for, um, the application not being approved was because of the lack of lending and it didn't meet the business activities of a bank as they, they, um, interpreted. There was, um, some secondary, um, notes and the secondary notes was that the capital that we proposed was not sufficient.

We didn't go into that in detail, but to, um, for others there's a $30 million initial capital requirement. There's been some discussion about that being lowered, especially I think for lower risk, um, entities, we propose 5 million. Um, there has been talk between being five and 10 million, but that 5 million proposal was declined.

Okay. Thank you. That's very helpful. 'cause I think there's some things we can follow up. Yeah. Okay. Anyone else? No. Okay. Thank you Mr. Marsh from dos. We appreciate your, uh, written submission and appearing before the committee, um, six months after we reported back on the banking inquiry. Great. Thanks. Along 2020. Thank you. Thank you. Okay.

Members, that leads us to kiwibank and we've got the chief executive and chair here, so, so, um, welcome, uh, Susan Peterson and Steve, uh, Mina. Good morning, marina. Thank you for joining us this morning and, uh, and rolling with our, um, timetabling.

I'm very happy to do so. Can we leave it at that? Yeah. Anyway, good morning. We're in your hands. Uh, we've got your submission if you're able to give us some, um, introductory remarks, and then we'll crack into some questions. Thank You. Yeah, no, thank you. And Marina, um, good morning to everybody and thank you chair and thank you members of the committee for, um, inviting us here to speak today.

We welcome this opportunity and I really wanna make it clear that we very much support your work to increase competition in this country. Um, in terms of, we've taken your lead in terms of res, um, responding with some very succinct answers to your questions. We, but we welcome the opportunity really to open that up, um, in any way you wanna take it forward.

I just wanted to say, look, it's obvious I'm new to Kiwibank. Um, I'm also new to Kiwi Group Capital. I was appointed to both boards early in December and, but I'm not new to financial services. And in terms of making that quite clear, I've had a long history at A NZ. I've also been on the board of a SB and more broader, more broadly in terms of financial services.

I've seen institutional banking, institutional equities, and private wealth management through Craig's. I've also been on the board of Xero for coming up nine years. So I understand FinTech and I understand how that fits together. So why Kiwi Bank? Um, I couldn't be more attracted to the purpose right now for our country. The idea of Kiwi making kiwi better off really resonates.

It resonates probably with us all in this room and looking forward to leaning in and having a few more folk of it lean into this opportunity to maximize and optimize the opportunity ahead of this, of this company for our country. So I wasn't really gonna say anything more than that. Um, but otherwise, hand it over to Steve in terms of taking, taking the questions that we've got and any, any inquiry you've got for, for us, just Welcome to the committee's questions. Oh, Thank you. Thank you. We're gonna go to Barbara Edmonds.

Yeah, good morning. And welcome. Um, just given your introductory comments and you do come into Kiwibank with some fresh eyes. Yes. What do you see are the opportunities or the opportunities that you know, that you're currently working through, where in particular your skillset in the FinTech area, how that can help Kiwi Bank advance their competition with the big banks?

Yeah, and it's a, it is a great question. I mean, the re the, the opportunity that I see here for Kiwi Bank is sort of, there's three growth vectors that sit in the operating side of the company. One is to grow, so to help more kiwi with, um, their kiwi living needs and finding a way to, to deliver on that. The second one is making sure that, um, you know, we can, you know, continue to attract the support we need to be able to inform that growth.

And then how can we run our bank in a way that is operationally efficient, that really push pressures on, on margins from the other banks. And I think among all those three growth vectors, I see opportunities specifically for FinTech. One of those opportunities, I guess, is to use technology to democratize access to more, um, features and facilities and products for more Kiwi.

Um, but that can be us doing it, but it can be partnering it, it can be networks, it can be done and to scale up in a way that doesn't rely us to do everything, but we can still give access to Kiwi to more. Yeah. And, um, Steve, more for you, obviously the capital requirement rules have, um, had a significant impact for Kiwi Bank. Um, you say in your, um, the memo to the select committee in the short term, or is it medium term?

What, um, can you just explain why just the short term and, and what would you need to look for to have a bit more, um, certainty for the medium and longer term in relation to capital requirements? Yeah, I mean, we are very positive about the changes. I think they reflect a long held view that the playing field hadn't been level level one. Uh, and so for smaller players, we had suffered significant disadvantage under the previous capital rule.

So I think there has been a, you know, really positive step towards leveling it. There's still, um, there's still some gaps that I, I don't follow the logic in. Um, so from, from a couple of areas. One is, um, if you believe that the internal models of the Big four banks are so much more superior than the standardized approach to capital, it's very hard to understand why Kiwibank carries half the impairments of any of the big four.

Mm-hmm. You know, so we are 40% of a N Z's impairments and 60% of the average of the big four. So I don't, I don't think it stands true to say that it's safer or that the risk is different if we were to, you know, lend to you versus one of the others. So I think that is a step in the right direction. I think under the, um, um, as we said in our answers under the depositors compensation scheme, where there is a, there's a sort of a weird window where if things don't change, we will be complying with the previous capital rules under the depositors

compensation scheme rather than the new rules. Okay. That will see us carrying more capital, even though we are meeting the requirements and exceeding the requirements. So I don't think that's, the logic doesn't flow to me, but that might be underway and, and gonna be changed. I think. I think the second thing is the, you know, there's some other technical elements, but I won't use the committee's time on that. So look, positive steps in the right direction.

I think the relief that, um, comes from a better view of risk weighted assets and the capital requirements means that we have about either $20 billion of home loans we could do, or sort of cir $7 billion of business banking, or a mix of both. Um, that would certainly give us really good, clear runway for the next two or three years, Barbara.

But after that, I think, you know, logically, Kiwi Group Capital and other stakeholders would be asking us, what does the future look like? And we don't want you to slow, uh, your growth because the above market growth means that, we'll, you be eating into that. Okay. Okay. I'm Gonna go Todd Stevenson. Uh, just A couple of questions from me. Um, uh, I just wanna push, I'm pushing all the bank shareholders of, um, payments New Zealand a bit harder.

No, thanks. I know. Thanks for coming by the way, first, and thanks for responses. Sure. But, um, I mean, you basically just said, look, they're carrying out some work, they're gonna do something. Um, you know, and again, I know it's a difficult 'cause you're obviously a, a user and a shareholder of payments New Zealand, but, you know, where do you think they're at? Can they be pushed a bit harder to kind of, um, overhaul their governance and really open up what they're doing and Yeah. What's your views on That, really? Yeah, look, I, I

think the views are that they're very awake to the fact that there is, the world is changing fast. Um, and that they need to change with the times. I mean, the, the, it's a bit of a conundrum with payments because you want 'em to be this most stable and the most safe thing you can do, as everyone on the committee will understand. You also understand that, um, customer's expectations are real time.

What has been very unusual in the last couple of years has been for, certainly for the length of time I've been in banking more than 25 years, we have been trying to take friction out of payments. Mm-hmm. And in the last two years, we're putting friction back into payments. Mm-hmm. Confirmation of payee and, and phishing and, and scams. So, um, I think all of us are facing into, you know, a balancing act.

Um, I think it's very difficult to, um, be adaptable and agile when you've got such a broad range of stakeholders and, and shareholders. Look, not everyone's on the same page. Yeah. So, look, I think there's work to do, certainly my conversation. Um, and, um, with the previous chair with, um, payments New Zealanders, we hear the public's voice loud and clear that we need to change and adjust.

Obviously we try and push that with our voice, but we're not the biggest player at the table. Um, we think that the opening up and modernization, because we are, you know, sort of two thirds of the way or halfway through our transformation, we'd like to see that accelerate. So our view would be, you know, um, modernizing getting a clear roadmap of what that looks like and, and bringing some new capability to the mixes as much required.

So, and then just my other quick one was, um, you kind of touched on it, your transformation. Obviously you're not quite, um, subject to open banking yet, but it's coming up pretty soon. Yeah. How's, how's your transformation going? Are you gonna be ready for open banking? Just a quick update. Yeah, absolutely. We're gonna meet our deadlines. Um, you know, we are, we are, puffing is a good way to say we're, we're running pretty hard.

So we will be delivering, uh, on the 29th of May for, uh, you know, one June deadline. So if you, I mean, the nature of why we've had to do it the way we are is because we are basically changing and transforming basically wing to wink at the bank. Mm-hmm. If we had built into the previous technology, we would've had to rip it out and put it back in.

That doesn't make any sense at all what I would say the outcomes of open banking and what you would hope for. I mean, Kiwibank has been offering a FinTech connection for lending since 2019. We are up for being more open and as the smallest player, we, we are a net beneficiary. So all it is is just a logical sense.

And I think I've told the committee previously, you know, if you're a homeowner, you wouldn't repaint the part of the house you're about to demolish. And that's really just the simple logic, which is, you know, we will be ready to go. Um, I think we are gonna be in a, an advantage in a sense, which is, we're coming a little bit later, we can see what's unfolding. The platform partner we've chosen, um, I think is in a more modern and contemporary technology architecture than the previous, um, iteration of open banking.

That's not, um, that's not to be critical of the big banks 'cause they had to go sooner. So they've had to connect to 40-year-old technology. And I'm sure you'll hear from the big banks later on, that's not a very pleasant experience to try and make modern tech. Talk to some of the, you know, I know firsthand from one of the banks that that call that they're talking about is 40 years old. Thank you. Okay. So we're on the 1st of June. Mm-hmm. Uh, we're gonna go to, Um, Dr. Megan Woods.

Thank you. Just, um, two questions. One of them very quick. Um, Steve, you talked about some technical changes that you won't, um, thank you. Um, but, um, in terms of that ability, um, around the, the $20 billion growth in the, the home lender or in the personal banking, 7 billion in business banking, are there any tweaks that can be made within those technical changes that we need to be aware of that would even increase that pool?

I think the gap between, um, us using standardized risk wa weighted assets and the way that the big banks are able to use their internal models still does give them an advantage. It's a much reduced advantage. So I don't wanna seem ish about the, you know, how we've moved, but there is still a gap there, which, you know, an asset is an asset and its risk is its risk. So I think there's still that gap there.

I think importantly for us, the deposit is compensation scheme is the one that we really do need to have focus on. Because if we can't, if we are at a disadvantage via the levy, means that we will not be as competitive as we need to be overall, or we won't be, um, as competitive as we need to be in attracting deposits. If we can't attract the deposits, then our cost of funding is higher and we are less competitive.

You know, that's obviously the way that it unfolds. And so I think that is really important that, um, the risk for the depositors is reflected in the, in the way that the levy works. So that's the, that's the one I would like to focus On. So does the, the, the, the committee, the kites, do they have the details of this? Yeah, they Do. Yep. Fabulous. So that, that would be good.

'cause it is those technical things we are looking for. The other thing is on your scheme. Yep. Um, thank you for doing that. I think it's got the potential to unlock a huge amount of possibility in New Zealand. Um, what's the kind of growth you are seeing across that? Yeah, look, it's, um, it's a slower burn than we would hope for.

Yeah. Um, we certainly get a lot of inquiry. What we, what we're really focused on now is we feel like we've got the right policy and the right infrastructure in play. Now. It's about getting a couple of hundred or, you know, two, 300 lenders in our business really familiar with how it works. Because often a a banker may only see that opportunity once every six months.

Yeah. Yeah. They're not as familiar as they can be about, oh, I remember how it works. I'm, I'm very hopeful that, um, this is where, you know, the use of, uh, a new generation of technology around AI that can assist the lenders and borrowers will mean that our borrowers a lot more comfortable and a lot more confident about what they offer. Because, you know, like anything in life, if you're only doing it once every six months, you're not just confident as you could be.

Yeah. Yeah. Um, so we are working really hard in the Maori, um, part of our business, the team there. We've changed the leadership model there with a very experienced commercial and retail lender to lead that business. Um, I'm very confident that Wayne has got the skills to really drive the awareness. So I think we've got an awareness issue more than a policy issue.

Sure. Um, but we can do a lot more. Um, and I was really struck with, um, a couple of things I've attended late last year that there's such a big opportunity. It's the fastest growing youngest generation of Al Tiera. So the opportunity is very big for, for banks and all financial services providers. Um, but we sort of need to get out of our own way a bit, um, and think differently about, particularly about Maori freehold land Yeah.

Which is, uh, an underutilized asset and liberate a lot of capital. So yeah, I think all of it, all of the banks would, would give you the answer that we can do more and we should do more. Yeah. I mean, for, for years the government had sitting there as, you know, an ability to give home grants Yeah. Based on, and it just couldn't be utilized.

No, that's right. Yeah. Um, so it is hugely, one of the things I'm interested in. Do you see the potential in that through individuals being able to use, uh, to be able to, um, borrow against the, uh, Eno Maori, um, assets. Mm-hmm. There, there's, there's shares in that, or do you see there's also an opportunity for, um, larger groups of people whanau to, to develop Papa Kaga and to use it as more of a collective? I think Economically the answers both.

Yeah. Okay. I mean, I think we, we just have to be a lot more flexible about the way that we approach it. And I, and I actually think as you think about, um, you know, many situations where a lender and a bank is lending to someone, you know, for instance, the school Yeah. You have to realistically understand that you're not gonna enforce the security. Mm-hmm. Yeah. That's not the reason that you are lending someone that money, but banks are wired to do whatever is the, you know, fits the box and fits the policy most easily.

So I think, you know, it's, it's very fair that the committee challenges is all to look at that differently and to take a different approach. I mean, no one is more invested in the reputation and support and security of that eno than, than Iwi and the multi-generational approach. The problem with banks typically think quarters and months. Yeah. Not years and generations.

And so I think that's a challenge that quite rightly the committee would give us. I'm just gonna go to a Think. Thank you Dr. Woods. I'm gonna go to us up from the honorable Barbara Redmonds. Thank You. Just, yeah. Keeping on the same topic, um, a anecdotally, uh, what I'm observing when I speak to particularly ua, uh, um, hapu, you know, hapu so, or families that are actually basically trying to sort out their land, is that not necessarily that the banks are, um, banks are ready to lend because you've set up your framework,

but actually for them to get to that point and to structure their own affairs, including the fact that the succession rules are so difficult for them to set up a trust, that's what I'm here, what I'm observing anecdotally, and yeah, I've had a number of particular cases like that. Is that, uh, is that something that your lenders are observing as well?

Is that they're coming to you saying, we've got land? Yeah. But that's about it. Yeah. Yeah. I mean, the, the ownership structure is, is challenging, as you say. Yeah. I also think there's probably, um, on the banker side of the table, there's not a clear appreciation of, of that complexity when you start.

Yeah. And so as it unfolds, you might think that the, the shape and the, and the arrangements are reasonably clear and they get more complex and they unfold that way. Mm-hmm. Um, I certainly know from a commercial lending perspective, I've been in situations where we've thought we've had agreement and, and unity, and then it's changed over time. So I think that's, you know, that's natural as well.

Um, so yes is the answer. Um, I think we could do more around, um, maybe as an NZBA level or some coalition level to try and help people and support people to give them a more of a framework and a pathway to how ownership could be clarified.

Um, but I think realistically there has to be done in partnership with Hapu. I think we haven't done well when we've tried to, you know, look, this is the way we think it should work and, and, and take this. And I mean, I I I think the other part of the equation is the Maori Land Corps and the fact that there are a lot of delays in there for those that wanting to get the interview.

Yeah. I think more resourcing there would de definitely help in a system. Very good. We're gonna go to Dan bid. Thank you Steve and, uh, Susan for joining us today. Um, in your response to submission around the regulatory overlap, you've put a note here about ensuring there are dis uh, there are discipline boundaries between their respective responsibilities of all the regulators.

Mm-hmm. Um, clearly there are a lot of regulators and overlaps and responsibilities. Um, my question to you is, uh, are, is kiwibank able to proactively provide areas of opportunity reducing those overlaps to the council of financial regulators?

Or how, how do you give that upward feedback to me carefully? Um, it's, it is a, it is a great question. They, we feel like we give the feedback pretty loud and clear. Yes. Um, we have a very detailed schematic of the amount that's on the go, and it's overlaps.

Um, and it's certainly enough to give you a migraine when you look at it. Um, the challenge we find ultimately is I think there's a good intention to prevent the overlap.
